In the one-step case this is equivalent to performing score matching, as Лңx = gО·(z1) with z1 вҲј N(0, I), and zs вҲј q(zs|Лңx) is sampled using forward diffusion starting from Лңx. Recall here that q(zs|Лңx, z1) = q(zs|Лңx) as z1 is pure noise, uncorrelated with zs. Upon convergence, weвҖҷll have that (ОұsЛҶxП•(zs) вҲ’ zs)/Пғ2 s = EpО·(Лңx|zs)[(ОұsЛңx вҲ’ zs)/Пғ2 s] = вҲҮzs log pО·(zs) вҲҖzs, i.e. our auxiliary model will match the score of the marginal sampling distribution of the distilled model pО·(zs) (because the optimal solution is ЛҶxП•(zs) = EЛңxвҲјpО· [Лңx]). In the multi-step case this equivalence does not hold, since the sampling distribution of zs depends on zt. Instead the proper multistep score is given by: вҲҮzs log pО·(zs) = EpО·(Лңx,zt|zs)[вҲҮzs log pО·(zs|Лңx, zt)] (18) = EpО·(Лңx,zt|zs)[(ВөtвҶ’s(Лңx, zt) вҲ’ zs)/Пғ2 tвҶ’s], (19) with Пғ2 tвҶ’s =  1 Пғ2s + Оұ2 t|s Пғ2 t|s вҲ’1 and ВөtвҶ’s = Пғ2 tвҶ’s Оұt|s Пғ2 t|s zt + Оұs Пғ2s Лңx  . (20) This expression suggests we could perform score matching by denoising towards ВөtвҶ’s(Лңx, zt), a linear combination of Лңx and zt, rather than just towards Лңx. We tried this in early experiments, but did not get good results. However, moment matching can still be seen to match the proper score expression (equation 19) approximately, if we assume that the forward processes match, meaning pО·(zt|zs) вүҲ q(zt|zs). This then gives: вҲҮzs log pО·(zs) вүҲ EpО·(Лңx|zs)q(zt|zs)[(ВөtвҶ’s(Лңx, zt) вҲ’ zs)/Пғ2 tвҶ’s] (21) = EpО·(Лңx|zs)q(zt|zs) hОұt|s Пғ2 t|s zt + Оұs Пғ2s Лңx вҲ’  1 Пғ2s + Оұ2 t|s Пғ2 t|s  zs i (22) = EpО·(Лңx|zs) hОұ2 t|s Пғ2 t|s zs + Оұs Пғ2s Лңx вҲ’  1 Пғ2s + Оұ2 t|s Пғ2 t|s  zs i (23) = EpО·(Лңx|zs) hОұs Пғ2s Лңx вҲ’ 1 Пғ2s zs i (24) = ( ОұsEpО· [Лңx|zs] вҲ’ zs)/Пғ2 s. (25) When this is used to fit the auxiliary score sП•(zs) = (ОұsЛҶxП•(zs) вҲ’ zs)/Пғ2 s, it is equivalent to fitting ЛҶxП•(zs) against just Лңx, so under this approximation moment matching and score matching once again become equivalent. If our distilled model has pО·(Лңx|zt) = q(x|zt), and if zt вҲј q(zt) (which is true during training), the approximation pО·(zt|zs) вүҲ q(zt|zs) would become exact. Both multistep moment matching and multistep score matching thus have a fixed point that corresponds to the correct target distribution q. We currently do not have any results on guaranteeing when this fixed point is indeed attained for both methods, and exploring this further would make for useful future research. Note that in general pО·(zt|zs) Мё= q(zt|zs) until convergence, so during optimization moment matching and score matching indeed optimize different objectives. 17

llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Introduces Moment Matching Distillation (MMD), a framework to distill many-step diffusion models into few-step generators by matching conditional expectations of clean data given noisy data along the sampling trajectory. Provides two practical algorithms: (1) Alternating optimization with an auxiliary denoising model and (2) an instant, parameter-space variant that matches expected teacher gradients using two independent minibatches. Demonstrates state-of-the-art few-step sampling on ImageNet (64Г—64 and 128Г—128), with 8-step distilled models outperforming their 1024-step teachers. Extends one-step score/distillation methods to multi-step settings via a generalized method-of-moments perspective, and shows promising results on large pixel-space text-to-image generation at 512Г—512 without autoencoders/upsamplers.
llm_extracted_info.methodology:
- Core idea: Distill by enforcing moment conditions Eg[ x~ | zs ] = Eq[ x | zs ], where x~ is the generatorвҖҷs clean prediction and zs is a noisy latent along the trajectory. Optimize an L2 distance between these conditional expectations while sampling zs from q(zs | zt, x~) (conditional on teacherвҖҷs latent zt).
- Alternating optimization (Algorithm 2): Train an auxiliary denoising model g_П• on samples from the distilled generator to approximate Eg[ x~ | zs ], regularized toward the teacher g_Оё. Alternate updates: (a) fit g_П• with standard diffusion loss plus regularization to teacher; (b) update generator g_О· with loss w(s) x~^T stopgrad[g_П•(zs) вҲ’ g_Оё(zs)]. Trains over continuous times t with random Оҙt and uses conditional q(zs|zt, x~).
- Instant parameter-space moment matching (Algorithm 3): Eliminate the auxiliary model by approximating it with a single infinitesimal gradient step from teacher parameters and matching teacher gradients (Efficient Method of Moments view). Optimize the squared norm of the difference between expected teacher gradients on generated vs real data: minimize 1/2 ||EвҲҮ_Оё L_Оё(x~, zs) вҲ’ EвҲҮ_Оё L_Оё(x, zвҖІs)||^2_Оӣ using Jacobian-vector products; requires two independent minibatches. Uses Adam-preconditioner Оӣ loaded from the teacher.
- Sampling: Standard ancestral sampler over k steps (kвҲҲ[1,8]); during training draw s from EDM schedule p(s), use w(s) (EDM or flatter for very small k), and a ОҙtвҲјU[0,1/k]. Supports classifier-free guidance (straight-through gradient through teacher when enabled).
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup:
- Datasets/Tasks: ImageNet class-conditional generation at 64Г—64 and 128Г—128; large-scale text-to-image at 512Г—512 using a licensed text-image dataset; zero-shot evaluation on MS-COCO for text-to-image.
- Models: Teacher and student use UViT architecture (Simple Diffusion). ImageNet teacher: ~400M params, 1024 denoising steps; distilled students evaluated at kвҲҲ{1,2,4,8}. Text-to-image model: pixel-space UViT with T5-XXL text encoder; no autoencoders/upsamplers.
- Training details: Distillation up to 200k steps (ImageNet) with global batch 2048; text-to-image up to 50k steps with batch 512. Hardware: TPU v5e, 256 chips. Optimizer: Adam (ОІ1=0, ОІ2=0.99, Оө=1eвҲ’12), 1k warmup then linear decay, grad clip norm 1, no EMA/weight decay/dropout. For instant method, fix Оӣ from teacherвҖҷs Adam stats. Time sampling uses EDM schedule; for kвҲҲ{1,2} use flatter w(s) for stability.
- Evaluation: FID and Inception Score on ImageNet; zero-shot FID (30k) and CLIP Score on MS-COCO for text-to-image. Fixed seeds and evaluation sets; report best FID during distillation.
- Key results: ImageNet 64Г—64 вҖ” Teacher FID 1.42; MMD (8-step) FID 1.24 (alternating), 1.35 (instant). ImageNet 128Г—128 вҖ” Teacher FID 1.76; MMD (8-step) FID 1.49 (alternating), 1.54 (instant). Inception Scores remain strong and increase with guidance. Text-to-image 512Г—512 вҖ” Base (512 steps): FID 9.6 (no guidance), 7.9 (0.5 guidance); MMD (8-step alternating): FID 7.25 (no guidance), CLIP 0.297; with guidance 3, CLIP improves but FID degrades. Ablations show conditional q(zs|zt, x~) improves sample diversity over unconditional noise application; guidance increases IS/CLIP but can worsen FID.
llm_extracted_info.limitations:
- Alternating optimization resembles GAN training and lacks convergence guarantees; stability sensitive to hyperparameters and improves with larger k (e.g., kвүҘ8). One-step performance trails some specialized baselines.
- Instant (parameter-space) variant performs worse at very small k and requires two independent minibatches and JacobianвҖ“vector products; theoretical analysis is complex and relies on teacher at (near) optimality (zero expected gradient) and fixed preconditioner Оӣ.
- Objective uses stop-gradient approximations (ignores dependence of zs on О·) and approximate moment estimates; Eg[ x~ | zs ] is not tractable.
- Strong computational requirements (large-scale TPUs, large batches). Evaluation relies on automated metrics (FID/IS/CLIP) without human studies.
- Requires careful weighting schedules (w(s)) for small k; broader applicability beyond images and specific architectures not fully validated.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions:
- Improve performance in the 1-step regime and understand stability/quality trade-offs for very small k.
- Theoretical analysis and convergence guarantees for both alternating and instant variants; characterize fixed points and conditions under which moment matching equals score matching in multistep settings.
- Human evaluations to complement FID/IS/CLIP; perceptual studies across datasets and resolutions.
- Learn or adapt the gradient preconditioner Оӣ, explore layer-wise/structure-aware preconditioning, and reduce two-batch overhead.
- Extend to other modalities (video, audio, 3D), higher resolutions, and conditional tasks; integrate with deterministic samplers (e.g., DDIM) and improved time/step schedules.
- Analyze why distilled models can surpass teachers; design objectives that explicitly exploit cross-time inconsistencies or error patterns in the teacher.
- Efficiency: reduce compute via curriculum over k, adaptive time deltas, or knowledge distillation cascades; investigate semi-/self-supervised variants and data efficiency.

research_study_list.title: DPM-Solver: A Fast ODE Solver for Diffusion Probabilistic Model Sampling in Around 10 Steps
research_study_list.abstract:
Diffusion probabilistic models (DPMs) are emerging powerful generative
models. Despite their high-quality generation performance, DPMs still suffer
from their slow sampling as they generally need hundreds or thousands of
sequential function evaluations (steps) of large neural networks to draw a
sample. Sampling from DPMs can be viewed alternatively as solving the
corresponding diffusion ordinary differential equations (ODEs). In this work,
we propose an exact formulation of the solution of diffusion ODEs. The
formulation analytically computes the linear part of the solution, rather than
leaving all terms to black-box ODE solvers as adopted in previous works. By
applying change-of-variable, the solution can be equivalently simplified to an
exponentially weighted integral of the neural network. Based on our
formulation, we propose DPM-Solver, a fast dedicated high-order solver for
diffusion ODEs with the convergence order guarantee. DPM-Solver is suitable for
both discrete-time and continuous-time DPMs without any further training.
Experimental results show that DPM-Solver can generate high-quality samples in
only 10 to 20 function evaluations on various datasets. We achieve 4.70 FID in
10 function evaluations and 2.87 FID in 20 function evaluations on the CIFAR10
dataset, and a $4\sim 16\times$ speedup compared with previous state-of-the-art
training-free samplers on various datasets.
research_study_list.full_text: DPM-Solver: A Fast ODE Solver for Diffusion Probabilistic Model Sampling in Around 10 Steps Cheng LuвҖ , Yuhao ZhouвҖ , Fan BaoвҖ , Jianfei ChenвҖ вҲ—, Chongxuan LiвҖЎ, Jun ZhuвҖ вҲ— вҖ Dept. of Comp. Sci. & Tech., Institute for AI, BNRist Center, THBI Lab вҖ Tsinghua-Bosch Joint ML Center, Tsinghua University, Beijing, 100084 China вҖЎGaoling School of Artiп¬Ғcial Intelligence, Renmin University of China, вҖЎBeijing Key Laboratory of Big Data Management and Analysis Methods, Beijing, China {lucheng.lc15, yuhaoz.cs}@gmail.com; bf19@mails.tsinghua.edu.cn chongxuanli@ruc.edu.cn; {jianfeic, dcszj}@tsinghua.edu.cn Abstract Diffusion probabilistic models (DPMs) are emerging powerful generative models. Despite their high-quality generation performance, DPMs still suffer from their slow sampling as they generally need hundreds or thousands of sequential function evaluations (steps) of large neural networks to draw a sample. Sampling from DPMs can be viewed alternatively as solving the corresponding diffusion ordinary differential equations (ODEs). In this work, we propose an exact formulation of the solution of diffusion ODEs. The formulation analytically computes the linear part of the solution, rather than leaving all terms to black-box ODE solvers as adopted in previous works. By applying change-of-variable, the solution can be equivalently simpliп¬Ғed to an exponentially weighted integral of the neural network. Based on our formulation, we propose DPM-Solver, a fast dedicated high-order solver for diffusion ODEs with the convergence order guarantee. DPM-Solver is suitable for both discrete-time and continuous-time DPMs without any further training. Experimental results show that DPM-Solver can generate high-quality samples in only 10 to 20 function evaluations on various datasets. We achieve 4.70 FID in 10 function evaluations and 2.87 FID in 20 function evaluations on the CIFAR10 dataset, and a 4 вҲј16Г—speedup compared with previous state-of-the-art training-free samplers on various datasets.2 1 Introduction Diffusion probabilistic models (DPMs) [1вҖ“3] are emerging powerful generative models with promis- ing performance on many tasks, such as image generation [4, 5], video generation [6], text-to-image generation [7], speech synthesis [8, 9] and lossless compression [10]. DPMs are deп¬Ғned by discrete- time random processes [1, 2] or continuous-time stochastic differential equations (SDEs) [3], which learn to gradually remove the noise added to the data points. Compared with the widely-used genera- tive adversarial networks (GANs) [11] and variational auto-encoders (V AEs) [12], DPMs can not only compute exact likelihood [3], but also achieve even better sample quality for image generation [4]. However, to obtain high-quality samples, DPMs usually need hundreds or thousands of sequential steps of large neural network evaluations, thereby resulting in a much slower sampling speed than the single-step GANs or V AEs. Such inefп¬Ғciency is becoming a critical bottleneck for the adoption of DPMs in downstream tasks, leading to an urgent request to design fast samplers for DPMs. вҲ—Corresponding Author. 2Code is available at https://github.com/LuChengTHU/dpm-solver 36th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2022). arXiv:2206.00927v3  [cs.LG]  13 Oct 2022NFE = 10  NFE = 15  NFE = 20  NFE = 100  NFE = 10 (a) DDIM [19] (b) DPM-Solver (ours) Figure 1: Samples by DDIM [19] with 10, 15, 20, 100 number of function evaluations (NFE), and DPM-Solver (ours) with only 10 NFE, using the pre-trained DPMs on ImageNet 256Г—256 with classiп¬Ғer guidance [4]. Existing fast samplers for DPMs can be divided into two categories. The п¬Ғrst category includes knowledge distillation [13, 14] and noise level or sample trajectory learning [15вҖ“18]. Such methods require a possibly expensive training stage before they can be used for efп¬Ғcient sampling. Furthermore, their applicability and п¬Ӯexibility might be limited. It might require nontrivial effort to adapt the method to different models, datasets, and number of sampling steps. The second category consists of training-free [19вҖ“21] samplers, which are suitable for all pre-trained DPMs in a simple plug-and- play manner. Training-free samplers include adopting implicit [ 19] or analytical [ 21] generation process, advanced differential equation (DE) solvers [3, 20, 22вҖ“24] and dynamic programming [18]. However, these methods still require вҲј50 function evaluations [21] to generate high-quality samples (comparable to those generated by plain samplers in about 1000 function evaluations), thereby are still time-consuming. In this work, we bring the efп¬Ғciency of training-free samplers to a new level to produce high-quality samples in the вҖңfew-step samplingвҖқ regime, where the sampling can be done within around 10 steps of sequential function evaluations. We tackle the alternative problem of sampling from DPMs as solving the corresponding diffusion ordinary differential equations (ODEs) of DPMs, and carefully examine the structure of diffusion ODEs. Diffusion ODEs have a semi-linear structure вҖ” they consist of a linear function of the data variable and a nonlinear function parameterized by neural networks. Such structure is omitted in previous training-free samplers [ 3, 20], which directly use black-box DE solvers. To utilize the semi-linear structure, we derive an exact formulation of the solutions of diffusion ODEs by analytically computing the linear part of the solutions, avoiding the corresponding discretization error. Furthermore, by applying change-of-variable, the solutions can be equivalently simpliп¬Ғed to an exponentially weighted integral of the neural network. Such integral is very special and can be efп¬Ғciently approximated by the numerical methods for exponential integrators [25]. Based on our formulation of solutions, we propose DPM-Solver, a fast dedicated solver for diffusion ODEs by approximating the above integral. Speciп¬Ғcally, we propose п¬Ғrst-order, second-order and third-order versions of DPM-Solver with convergence order guarantees. We further propose an adaptive step size schedule for DPM-Solver. In general, DPM-Solver is applicable to both continuous- time and discrete-time DPMs, and also conditional sampling with classiп¬Ғer guidance [ 4]. Fig. 1 demonstrates the speedup performance of a Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models (DDIM) [ 19] baseline and DPM-Solver, which shows that DPM-Solver can generate high-quality samples with as few as 10 function evaluations and is much faster than DDIM on the ImageNet 256x256 dataset [26]. Our additional experimental results show that DPM-Solver can greatly improve the sampling speed of both discrete-time and continuous-time DPMs, and it can achieve excellent sample quality in around 10 function evaluations, which is much faster than all previous training-free samplers of DPMs. 2 Diffusion Probabilistic Models We review diffusion probabilistic models and their associated differential equations in this section. 22.1 Forward Process and Diffusion SDEs Assume that we have a D-dimensional random variable x0 вҲҲRD with an unknown distribution q0(x0). Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DPMs) [1вҖ“3, 10] deп¬Ғne a forward process {xt}tвҲҲ[0,T] with T >0 starting with x0, such that for any tвҲҲ[0,T], the distribution of xt conditioned on x0 satisп¬Ғes q0t(xt|x0) = N(xt|Оұ(t)x0,Пғ2(t)I), (2.1) where Оұ(t),Пғ(t) вҲҲR+ are differentiable functions of twith bounded derivatives, and we denote them as Оұt,Пғt for simplicity. The choice for Оұt and Пғt is referred to as the noise schedule of a DPM. Let qt(xt) denote the marginal distribution of xt, DPMs choose noise schedules to ensure that qT(xT) вүҲN(xT|0,ЛңПғ2I) for some ЛңПғ >0, and the signal-to-noise-ratio (SNR) Оұ2 t/Пғ2 t is strictly decreasing w.r.t. t[10]. Moreover, Kingma et al. [10] prove that the following stochastic differential equation (SDE) has the same transition distribution q0t(xt|x0) as in Eq. (2.1) for any tвҲҲ[0,T]: dxt = f(t)xtdt+ g(t)dwt, x0 вҲјq0(x0), (2.2) where wt вҲҲRD is the standard Wiener process, and f(t) = d logОұt dt , g 2(t) = dПғ2 t dt вҲ’2d logОұt dt Пғ2 t. (2.3) Under some regularity conditions, Song et al. [3] show that the forward process in Eq. (2.2) has an equivalent reverse process from time T to 0, starting with the marginal distribution qT(xT): dxt = [f(t)xt вҲ’g2(t)вҲҮxlog qt(xt)]dt+ g(t)d ВҜwt, xT вҲјqT(xT), (2.4) where ВҜwt is a standard Wiener process in the reverse time. The only unknown term in Eq. (2.4) is the score function вҲҮxlog qt(xt) at each time t. In practice, DPMs use a neural network ПөОё(xt,t) parameterized by Оё to estimate the scaled score function: вҲ’ПғtвҲҮxlog qt(xt). The parameter Оё is optimized by minimizing the following objective [2, 3]: L(Оё; Пү(t)) := 1 2 вҲ« T 0 Пү(t)Eqt(xt) [ вҲҘПөОё(xt,t) + ПғtвҲҮxlog qt(xt)вҲҘ2 2 ] dt = 1 2 вҲ« T 0 Пү(t)Eq0(x0)Eq(Пө) [ вҲҘПөОё(xt,t) вҲ’ПөвҲҘ2 2 ] dt+ C, where Пү(t) is a weighting function, ПөвҲјq(Пө) = N(Пө|0,I), xt = Оұtx0 + ПғtПө, and C is a constant independent of Оё. As ПөОё(xt,t) can also be regarded as predicting the Gaussian noise added to xt, it is usually called the noise prediction model. Since the ground truth of ПөОё(xt,t) is вҲ’ПғtвҲҮxlog qt(xt), DPMs replace the score function in Eq. (2.4) by вҲ’ПөОё(xt,t)/Пғt and deп¬Ғne a parameterized reverse process (diffusion SDE) from time T to 0, starting with xT вҲјN(0,ЛңПғ2I): dxt = [ f(t)xt + g2(t) Пғt ПөОё(xt,t) ] dt+ g(t)d ВҜwt, xT вҲјN(0,ЛңПғ2I). (2.5) Samples can be generated from DPMs by solving the diffusion SDE in Eq. (2.5) with numerical solvers, which discretize the SDE from T to 0. Song et al. [3] proved that the traditional ancestral sampling method for DPMs [2] can be viewed as a п¬Ғrst-order SDE solver for Eq.(2.5). However, these п¬Ғrst-order methods usually need hundreds of or thousands of function evaluations to converge [3], leading to extremely slow sampling speed. 2.2 Diffusion (Probability Flow) ODEs When discretizing SDEs, the step size is limited by the randomness of the Wiener process [27, Chap. 11]. A large step size (small number of steps) often causes non-convergence, especially in high dimensional spaces. For faster sampling, one can consider the associated probability п¬Ӯow ODE [3], which has the same marginal distribution at each time tas that of the SDE. Speciп¬Ғcally, for DPMs, Song et al. [3] proved that the probability п¬Ӯow ODE of Eq. (2.4) is dxt dt = f(t)xt вҲ’1 2g2(t)вҲҮxlog qt(xt), xT вҲјqT(xT), (2.6) 3where the marginal distribution of xt is also qt(xt). By replacing the score function with the noise prediction model, Song et al. [3] deп¬Ғned the following parameterized ODE (diffusion ODE): dxt dt = hОё(xt,t) := f(t)xt + g2(t) 2Пғt ПөОё(xt,t), xT вҲјN(0,ЛңПғ2I). (2.7) Samples can be drawn by solving the ODE from T to 0. Comparing with SDEs, ODEs can be solved with larger step sizes as they have no randomness. Furthermore, we can take advantage of efп¬Ғcient numerical ODE solvers to accelerate the sampling. Song et al. [3] used the RK45 ODE solver [28] for the diffusion ODEs, which generates samples in вҲј60 function evaluations to reach comparable quality with a 1000-step SDE solver for Eq. (2.5) on the CIFAR-10 dataset [29]. However, existing general-purpose ODE solvers still cannot generate satisfactory samples in the few-step (вҲј10 steps) sampling regime. To the best of our knowledge, there is still a lack of training-free samplers for DPMs in the few-step sampling regime, and the sampling speed of DPMs is still a critical issue. 3 Customized Fast Solvers for Diffusion ODEs As highlighted in Sec. 2.2, discretizing SDEs is generally difп¬Ғcult in high dimensions [27, Chap. 11] and it is hard to converge within few steps. In contrast, ODEs are easier to solve, yielding a potential for fast samplers. However, as mentioned in Sec. 2.2, the general black-box ODE solver used in previous work [3] empirically fails to converge in few steps. This motivates us to design a dedicated solver for diffusion ODEs to enable fast and high-quality few-step sampling. We start with a detailed investigation of the speciп¬Ғc structure of diffusion ODEs. 3.1 Simpliп¬Ғed Formulation of Exact Solutions of Diffusion ODEs The key insight of this work is that given an initial value xs at time s> 0, the solution xt at each time t < sof diffusion ODEs in Eq. (2.7) can be simpliп¬Ғed into a very special exact formulation which can be efп¬Ғciently approximated. Our п¬Ғrst key observation is that a part of the solution xt can be exactly computed by considering the particular structure of diffusion ODEs. The r.h.s. of diffusion ODEs in Eq. (2.7) consists of two parts: the part f(t)xt is a linear function of xt, and the other part g2(t) 2Пғt ПөОё(xt,t) is generally a nonlinear function of xt because of the neural network ПөОё(xt,t). This type of ODE is referred to as semi-linear ODE. The black-box ODE solvers adopted by previous work [ 3] are ignorant of this semi-linear structure as they take the whole hОё(xt,t) in Eq. (2.7) as the input, which causes discretization errors of both the linear and nonlinear term. We note that for semi-linear ODEs, the solution at time tcan be exactly formulated by the вҖңvariation of constantsвҖқ formula [30]: xt = e вҲ« t s f(П„)dП„xs + вҲ« t s ( e вҲ« t П„ f(r)drg2(П„) 2ПғП„ ПөОё(xП„,П„) ) dП„. (3.1) This formulation decouples the linear part and the nonlinear part. In contrast to black-box ODE solvers, the linear part is now exactly computed, which eliminates the approximation error of the linear term. However, the integral of the nonlinear part is still complicated because it couples the coefп¬Ғcients about the noise schedule (i.e., f(П„),g(П„),ПғП„) and the complex neural network ПөОё, which is still hard to approximate. Our second key observation is that the integral of the nonlinear part can be greatly simpliп¬Ғed by introducing a special variable. Let О»t := log(Оұt/Пғt) (one half of the log-SNR), then О»t is a strictly decreasing function of t(due to the deп¬Ғnition of DPMs as discussed in Sec. 2.1). We can rewrite g(t) in Eq. (2.3) as g2(t) = dПғ2 t dt вҲ’2d logОұt dt Пғ2 t = 2Пғ2 t (d logПғt dt вҲ’d logОұt dt ) = вҲ’2Пғ2 t dО»t dt . (3.2) Combining with f(t) = d logОұt/dtin Eq. (2.3), we can rewrite Eq. (3.1) as xt = Оұt Оұs xs вҲ’Оұt вҲ« t s (dО»П„ dП„ )ПғП„ ОұП„ ПөОё(xП„,П„)dП„. (3.3) As О»(t) = О»t is a strictly decreasing function of t, it has an inverse function tО»(В·) satisfying t = tО»(О»(t)). We further change the subscripts of xand ПөОё from tto О»and denote ЛҶxО» := xtО»(О»), ЛҶПөОё(ЛҶxО»,О») := ПөОё(xtО»(О»),tО»(О»)). Rewrite Eq. (3.3) by вҖңchange-of-variableвҖқ for О», then we have: 4Proposition 3.1 (Exact solution of diffusion ODEs). Given an initial value xs at time s >0, the solution xt at time tвҲҲ[0,s] of diffusion ODEs in Eq. (2.7) is: xt = Оұt Оұs xs вҲ’Оұt вҲ« О»t О»s eвҲ’О»ЛҶПөОё(ЛҶxО»,О»)dО». (3.4) We call the integral вҲ« eвҲ’О»ЛҶПөОё(ЛҶxО»,О»)dО» the exponentially weighted integral of ЛҶПөОё, which is very special and highly related to the exponential integrators in the literature of ODE solvers [25]. To the best of our knowledge, such formulation has not been revealed in prior work of diffusion models. Eq. (3.4) provides a new perspective for approximating the solutions of diffusion ODEs. Speciп¬Ғcally, given xs at time s, According to Eq. (3.4), approximating the solution at time tis equivalent to directly approximating the exponentially weighted integral of ЛҶПөОё from О»s to О»t, which avoids the error of the linear terms and is well-studied in the literature of exponential integrators [25, 31]. Based on this insight, we propose fast solvers for diffusion ODEs, as detailed in the following sections. 3.2 High-Order Solvers for Diffusion ODEs In this section, we propose high-order solvers for diffusion ODEs with convergence order guarantee by leveraging our proposed solution formulation Eq. (3.4). The proposed solvers and analysis are highly motivated by the methods of exponential integrators [25, 31] in the ODE literature. Speciп¬Ғcally, given an initial value xT at time T and M + 1 time steps {ti}M i=0 decreasing from t0 = T to tM = 0. Let Лңxt0 = xT be the initial value. The proposed solvers use M steps to iteratively compute a sequence {Лңxti}M i=0 to approximate the true solutions at time steps {ti}M i=0. In particular, the last iterate ЛңxtM approximates the true solution at time 0. In order to reduce the approximation error between ЛңxtM and the true solution at time 0, we need to reduce the approximation error for each Лңxti at every step [30]. Starting with the previous value ЛңxtiвҲ’1 at time tiвҲ’1, according to Eq. (3.4), the exact solution xtiвҲ’1вҶ’ti at time ti is given by xtiвҲ’1вҶ’ti = Оұti ОұtiвҲ’1 ЛңxtiвҲ’1 вҲ’Оұti вҲ« О»ti О»tiвҲ’1 eвҲ’О»ЛҶПөОё(ЛҶxО»,О»)dО». (3.5) Therefore, to compute the value Лңxti for approximating xtiвҲ’1вҶ’ti, we need to approximate the exponentially weighted integral ofЛҶПөОё from О»tiвҲ’1 to О»ti. Denote hi := О»tiвҲ’О»tiвҲ’1 , and ЛҶПө(n) Оё (ЛҶxО»,О») := dnЛҶПөОё(ЛҶxО»,О») dО»n as the n-th order total derivative of ЛҶПөОё(ЛҶxО»,О») w.r.t. О». For k вүҘ1, the (kвҲ’1)-th order Taylor expansion of ЛҶПөОё(ЛҶxО»,О») w.r.t. О»at О»tiвҲ’1 is ЛҶПөОё(ЛҶxО»,О») = kвҲ’1вҲ‘ n=0 (О»вҲ’О»tiвҲ’1 )n n! ЛҶПө(n) Оё (ЛҶxО»tiвҲ’1 ,О»tiвҲ’1 ) + O((О»вҲ’О»tiвҲ’1 )k), Substituting the above Taylor expansion into Eq. (3.5) yields xtiвҲ’1вҶ’ti= Оұti ОұtiвҲ’1 ЛңxtiвҲ’1 вҲ’Оұti kвҲ’1вҲ‘ n=0 ЛҶПө(n) Оё (ЛҶxО»tiвҲ’1 ,О»tiвҲ’1 ) вҲ« О»ti О»tiвҲ’1 eвҲ’О»(О»вҲ’О»tiвҲ’1 )n n! dО»+O(hk+1 i ), (3.6) where the integral вҲ« eвҲ’О»(О»вҲ’О»tiвҲ’1 )n n! dО»can be analytically computed by repeatedly applying ntimes of integration-by-parts (see Appendix B.2). Therefore, to approximate xtiвҲ’1вҶ’ti, we only need to approximate the n-th order total derivatives ЛҶПө(n) Оё (ЛҶxО»,О») for n вүӨkвҲ’1, which is a well-studied problem in the ODE literature [31, 32]. By dropping the O(hk+1 i ) error term and approximating the п¬Ғrst (kвҲ’1)-th total derivatives with the вҖңstiff order conditionsвҖқ [31, 32], we can derive k-th-order ODE solvers for diffusion ODEs. We name such solvers as DPM-Solver overall, and DPM-Solver-k for a speciп¬Ғc order k. Here we take k= 1 for demonstration. In this case, Eq. (3.6) becomes xtiвҲ’1вҶ’ti = Оұti ОұtiвҲ’1 ЛңxtiвҲ’1 вҲ’ОұtiПөОё(ЛңxtiвҲ’1 ,tiвҲ’1) вҲ« О»ti О»tiвҲ’1 eвҲ’О»dО»+ O(h2 i) = Оұti ОұtiвҲ’1 ЛңxtiвҲ’1 вҲ’Пғti(ehi вҲ’1)ПөОё(ЛңxtiвҲ’1 ,tiвҲ’1) + O(h2 i). 5By dropping the high-order error term O(h2 i), we can obtain an approximation for xtiвҲ’1вҶ’ti. As k= 1 here, we call this solver DPM-Solver-1, and the detailed algorithm is as following. DPM-Solver-1. Given an initial value xT and M + 1 time steps {ti}M i=0 decreasing from t0 = T to tM = 0. Starting with Лңxt0 = xT, the sequence {Лңxti}M i=1 is computed iteratively as follows: Лңxti = Оұti ОұtiвҲ’1 ЛңxtiвҲ’1 вҲ’Пғti(ehi вҲ’1)ПөОё(ЛңxtiвҲ’1 ,tiвҲ’1), where hi = О»ti вҲ’О»tiвҲ’1 . (3.7) For k вүҘ2, approximating the п¬Ғrst kterms of the Taylor expansion needs additional intermediate points between tand s[31]. The derivation is more technical so we defer it to Appendix B. Below we propose algorithms for k= 2,3 and name them as DPM-Solver-2 and DPM-Solver-3, respectively. Algorithm 1 DPM-Solver-2. The chosen intermediate points are (si, ui) for DPM-Solver-2 and (s2iвҲ’1,u2iвҲ’1) and (s2i,u2i) for DPM-Solver-3. As shown in the algorithm, DPM-Solver-krequires kfunction evaluations per step for k= 1,2,3. Despite the more expensive steps, higher-order solvers (k= 2,3) are usually more efп¬Ғcient since they require much fewer steps to converge, due to their higher convergence order. We show that DPM-Solver-kis k-th-order solver, as stated in the following theorem. The proof is in Appendix B. Theorem 3.2 (DPM-Solver-kas a k-th-order solver). Assume ПөОё(xt,t) follows the regularity condi- tions detailed in Appendix B.1, then for k= 1,2,3, DPM-Solver-kis a k-th order solver for diffusion ODEs, i.e., for the sequence {Лңxti}M i=1 computed by DPM-Solver-k, the approximation error at time 0 satisп¬Ғes ЛңxtM вҲ’x0 = O(hk max), where hmax = max1вүӨiвүӨM(О»ti вҲ’О»tiвҲ’1 ). Finally, solvers with kвүҘ4 need much more intermediate points as shown by previous work [31, 32] for exponential integrators. Therefore, we only consider kfrom 1 to 3 in this work, while leaving the solvers with higher kfor future study. 63.3 Step Size Schedule The proposed solvers in Sec. 3.2 need to specify the time steps {ti}M i=0 in advance. We propose two choices of the time step schedule. One choice is handcrafted, which is to uniformly split the interval [О»T, О»0], i.e. О»ti = О»T + i M(О»0 вҲ’О»T), i = 0,...,M . Note that this is different from previous work [2, 3] which chooses uniform steps for ti. Empirically, DPM-Solver with uniform time steps О»ti can already generate quite good samples in few steps, where results are listed in Appendix E. As the other choice, we propose an adaptive step size algorithm, which dynamically adjusts the step size by combining different orders of DPM-Solver. The adaptive algorithm is inspired by [20] and we defer its implementation details to Appendix C. For few-step sampling, we need to use up all the number of function evaluations (NFE). When the NFE is not divisible by 3, we п¬Ғrstly apply DPM-Solver-3 as much as possible, and then add a single step of DPM-Solver-1 or DPM-Solver-2 (dependent on the reminder of Kdivided by 3), as detailed in Appendix D. In the subsequent experiments, we use such combination of solvers with the uniform step size schedule for NFE вүӨ20, and otherwise the adaptive step size schedule. 3.4 Sampling from Discrete-Time DPMs Discrete-time DPMs [2] train the noise prediction model at N п¬Ғxed time steps {tn}N n=1, and the noise prediction model is parameterized by ЛңПөОё(xn,n) for n = 0 ,...,N вҲ’1, where each xn is corresponding to the value at timetn+1. We can transform the discrete-time noise prediction model to the continuous version by letting ПөОё(x,t) := ЛңПөОё(x,(NвҲ’1)t T ), for all xвҲҲRd,t вҲҲ[0,T]. Note that the input time of ЛңПөОё may not be integers, but we п¬Ғnd that the noise prediction model can still work well, and we hypothesize that it is because of the smooth time embeddings (e.g., position embeddings [2]). By such reparameterization, the noise prediction model can adopt the continuous-time steps as input, and thus we can also use DPM-Solver for fast sampling. 4 Comparison with Existing Fast Sampling Methods Here, we discuss the relationship and highlight the difference between DPM-Solver and existing ODE-based fast sampling methods for DPMs. We further brieп¬Ӯy discuss the advantage of training-free samplers over those training-based ones. 4.1 DDIM as DPM-Solver-1 Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models (DDIM) [19] design a deterministic method for fast sampling from DPMs. For two adjacent time steps tiвҲ’1 and ti, assume that we have a solution ЛңxtiвҲ’1 at time tiвҲ’1, then a single step of DDIM from time tiвҲ’1 to time ti is Лңxti = Оұti ОұtiвҲ’1 ЛңxtiвҲ’1 вҲ’Оұti (ПғtiвҲ’1 ОұtiвҲ’1 вҲ’Пғti Оұti ) ПөОё(ЛңxtiвҲ’1 ,tiвҲ’1). (4.1) Although motivated by entirely different perspectives, we show that the updates of DPM-Solver-1 and Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models (DDIM) [19] are identical. By the deп¬Ғnition of О», we have ПғtiвҲ’1 ОұtiвҲ’1 = eвҲ’О»tiвҲ’1 and Пғti Оұti = eвҲ’О»ti. Plugging these and hi = О»tiвҲ’О»tiвҲ’1 to Eq. (4.1) results in exactly a step of DPM-Solver-1 in Eq. (3.7). However, the semi-linear ODE formulation of DPM-Solver allows for principled generalization to higher-order solvers and convergence order analysis. Recent work [13] also show that DDIM is a п¬Ғrst-order discretization of diffusion ODEs by differenti- ating both sides of Eq. (4.1). However, they cannot explain the difference between DDIM and the п¬Ғrst-order Euler discretization of diffusion ODEs. In contrast, by showing that DDIM is a special case of DPM-Solver, we reveal that DDIM makes full use of the semi-linearity of diffusion ODEs, which explains its superiority over traditional Euler methods. 4.2 Comparison with Traditional Runge-Kutta Methods One can obtain a high-order solver by directly applying traditional explicit Runge-Kutta (RK) methods to the diffusion ODE in Eq. (2.7). Speciп¬Ғcally, RK methods write the solution of Eq. The approximation error of explicit RK methods depends on hОё, which consists of the error corresponding to both the linear term f(П„)xП„ and the nonlinear noise prediction model ПөОё. However, the error of the linear term may increase exponentially because the exact solution of the linear term has an exponential coefп¬Ғcient (as shown in Eq.(3.1)). There are many empirical evidence [25, 31] showing that directly using explicit RK methods for semi-linear ODEs may suffer from unstable numerical issues for large step size. We also demonstrate the empirical difference of the proposed DPM-Solver and the traditional explicit RK methods in Sec. 5.1, which shows that DPM-Solver have smaller discretization errors than the RK methods with the same order. 4.3 Training-based Fast Sampling Methods for DPMs Samplers that need extra training or optimization include knowledge distillation [13, 14], learning the noise level or variance [15, 16, 33], and learning the noise schedule or sample trajectory [17, 18]. Although the progressive distillation method [13] can obtain a fast sampler within 4 steps, it needs further training costs and loses part of the information in the original DPM (e.g., after distillation, the noise prediction model cannot predict the noise (score function) at every time step between [0,T]). In contrast, training-free samplers can keep all the information of the original model, and thereby can be directly extended to the conditional sampling by combining the original model and an external classiп¬Ғer [4] (e.g. see Appendix D for the conditional sampling with classiп¬Ғer guidance). Beyond directly designing fast samplers for DPMs, several works also propose novel types of DPMs which supports faster sampling. For instance, deп¬Ғning a low-dimensional latent variable for DPMs [34]; designing special diffusion processes with bounded score functions [ 35]; combining GANs with the reverse process of DPMs [36]. The proposed DPM-Solver may also be suitable for accelerating the sampling of these DPMs, and we leave them for future work. 5 Experiments In this section, we show that as a training-free sampler, DPM-Solver can greatly speedup the sampling of existing pre-trained DPMs, including both continuous-time and discrete-time ones, with both linear noise schedule [2, 19] and cosine noise schedule [16]. Unless explicitly mentioned, we always use the solver combination with the uniform step size schedule in Sec. 3.3 if the NFE budget is less than 20, and otherwise the DPM-Solver-3 with the adaptive step size schedule in Sec. 3.3. To get the strongest baseline, we use the quadratic step size for DDIM on CelebA, which has a better FID than that of the uniform step size in the original paper [19]. 5.1 Comparison with Continuous-Time Sampling Methods We п¬Ғrstly compare DPM-Solver with other continuous-time sampling methods for DPMs. The compared methods include the Euler-Maruyama discretization for diffusion SDEs [3], the adaptive step size solver for diffusion SDEs [20] and the RK methods for diffusion ODEs [3, 28] in Eq. (2.7). We compare these methods for sampling from a pre-trained continuous-time вҖңVP deepвҖқ model [3] on the CIFAR-10 dataset [29] with the linear noise schedule. Fig. 2a shows the efп¬Ғciency of compared solvers. We use uniform time steps with 50, 200, 1000 NFE for the diffusion SDE with Euler discretization, and vary the tolerance hyperparameter [3, 20] for the adaptive step size SDE solver [20] and RK45 ODE solver [28] to control the NFE. DPM-Solver can generate good sample quality within around 10 NFE, while other solvers have large discretization error even in 50 NFE, which shows that DPM-Solver can achieveвҲј5 speedup of the previous best solver. In particular, we achieve 4.70 FID with 10 NFE, 3.75 FID with 12 NFE, 3.24 FID with 15 NFE, and 2.87 FID with 20 NFE, which is the fastest sampler on CIFAR-10. As an ablation study, we also compare the second-order and third-order DPM-Solver and RK methods, as shown in Table 1. We compare RK methods for diffusion ODEs w.r.t. both timetin Eq. (2.7) and half-log-SNR О»by applying change-of-variable (see detailed formulations in Appendix E.1). The results show that given the same NFE, the sample quality of DPM-Solver is consistently better than RK methods with the same order. The superior efп¬Ғciency of DPM-Solver is particularly evident in the few-step regime under 15 NFE, where RK methods have rather large discretization errors. This is mainly because DPM-Solver analytically computes the linear term, avoiding the corresponding discretization error. Besides, the higher order DPM-Solver-3 converges faster than DPM-Solver-2, which matches the order analysis in Theorem 3.2. 5.2 Comparison with Discrete-Time Sampling Methods We use the method in Sec. 3.4 for using DPM-Solver in discrete-time DPMs, and then compare DPM-Solver with other discrete-time training-free samplers, including DDPM [ 2], DDIM [ 19], Analytic-DDPM [21], Analytic-DDIM [21], PNDM [22], FastDPM [38] and ItГҙ-Taylor [24]. We also compare with GGDM [18], which uses the same pre-trained model but needs further training for the sampling trajectory. We compare the sample quality by varying NFE from 10 to 1000. 9Speciп¬Ғcally, we use the discrete-time model trained by Lsimple in [2] on the CIFAR-10 dataset with linear noise schedule; the discrete-time model in [ 19] on CelebA 64x64 [ 39] with linear noise schedule; the discrete-time model trained by Lhybrid in [16] on ImageNet 64x64 [ 26] with cosine noise schedule; the discrete-time model with classiп¬Ғer guidance in [4] on ImageNet 128x128 [26] with linear noise schedule; the discrete-time model in [ 4] on LSUN bedroom 256x256 [ 40] with linear noise schedule. For the models trained on ImageNet, we only use their вҖңmeanвҖқ model and omit the вҖңvarianceвҖқ model. As shown in Fig. 2, on all datasets, DPM-Solver can obtain reasonable samples within 12 steps (FID 4.65 on CIFAR-10, FID 3.71 on CelebA 64x64 and FID 19.97 on ImageNet 64x64, FID 4.08 on ImageNet 128x128), which is 4 вҲј16Г—faster than the previous fastest training-free sampler. DPM-Solver even outperforms GGDM, which requires additional training. 6 Conclusions We tackle the problem of fast and training-free sampling from DPMs. We propose DPM-Solver, a fast dedicated training-free solver of diffusion ODEs for fast sampling of DPMs in around 10 steps of function evaluations. DPM-Solver leverages the semi-linearity of diffusion ODEs and it directly approximates a simpliп¬Ғed formulation of exact solutions of diffusion ODEs, which consists of an exponentially weighted integral of the noise prediction model. Inspired by numerical methods for exponential integrators, we propose п¬Ғrst-order, second-order and third-order DPM- Solver to approximate the exponentially weighted integral of noise prediction models with theoretical convergence guarantee. We propose both handcrafted and adaptive step size schedule, and apply DPM-Solver for both continuous-time and discrete-time DPMs. Our experimental results show that DPM-Solver can generate high-quality samples in around 10 function evaluations on various datasets, and it can achieve 4 вҲј16Г—speedup compared with previous state-of-the-art training-free samplers. Limitations and broader impact Despite the promising speedup performance, DPM-Solver is designed for fast sampling, which may be not suitable for accelerating the likelihood evaluations of DPMs. Besides, compared to the commonly-used GANs, diffusion models with DPM-Solver are still not fast enough for real-time applications. In addition, like other deep generative models, DPMs may be used to generate adverse fake contents, and the proposed solver may further amplify the potential undesirable inп¬Ӯuence of deep generative models for malicious applications. We show that both the maximum likelihood training and the sampling of diffusion models have invariance formulations that are independent of the noise schedule. A.1 Decoupling the Sampling Solution from the Noise Schedule In this section, we show that Proposition 3.1 can decouples the exact solutions of the diffusion ODEs from the speciп¬Ғc noise schedules (i.e. choice of the functions Оұt = Оұ(t) and Пғt = Пғ(t)). Namely, given a starting point О»s, a ending point О»t, an initial value ЛҶxО»s at О»s and a noise prediction model ЛҶПөОё, the solution of ЛҶxО»t is invariant of the noise schedule between О»s and О»t. We п¬Ғrstly consider the VP type diffusion models, which is equivalent to the original DDPM [2, 3]. For VP type diffusion models, we always have Оұ2 t + Пғ2 t = 1 , so deп¬Ғning the noise schedule is equivalent to deп¬Ғning the function Оұt = Оұ(t) (For example, DDPM [2] uses a noise schedule such that ОІ(t) = d logОұt dt is a linear function of t, and i-DDPM [ 16] uses a noise schedule such that ОІ(t) = d logОұt dt is a cosine function of t). As О»t = log Оұt вҲ’log Пғt, we have Оұt = вҲҡ 1 1+eвҲ’2О»t and Пғt = вҲҡ 1 1+e2О»t. Thus, we can directly compute theОұtand Пғtfor a givenО»t. (A.2) We should notice that the integrand eвҲ’О»ЛҶПөОё(ЛҶxО»,О») is a function of О», so its integral from О»s to О»t is only dependent on the starting point О»s, the ending point О»t and the function ЛҶПөОё, which is independent of the intermediate values. As other coefп¬Ғcients (ЛҶОұО»s and ЛҶОұО»t) are also only dependent on the starting point О»s and the ending point О»t, we can conclude that ЛҶxО»t is invariant of the speciп¬Ғc choice of the noise schedules. Intuitively, this is because we converts the original integral of time tin Eq. (3.1) to the integral of О», and the functions f(t) and g(t) are converted to an analytical formulation eвҲ’О», which is invariant to the speciп¬Ғc choices of f(t) and g(t). Finally, for other types of diffusion models (such as the VE type and the subVP type), they are all equivalent to the VP type by equivalently rescaling the noise prediction models, as proved in [10]. Therefore, the solutions of these types also have such property. 14In summary, Proposition 3.1 decouples the solution of diffusion ODEs from the noise schedules, which gives us an opportunity to design tailor-made samplers for DPMs. In fact, as shown in Sec. 3.2, the only approximation of the proposed DPM-Solver is about the Taylor expansion of the neural network ЛҶПөОё w.r.t. О», and DPM-Solver analytically computes other coefп¬Ғcients (which are corresponding to the speciп¬Ғc noise schedules). Intuitively, DPM-Solver keeps the known information as much as possible, and only approximates the intractable integral of the neural network, so it can generate comparable samples within much fewer steps. A.2 Choosing Time Steps for О»is Invariant to the Noise Schedule As mentioned in Appendix A.1, the formulation of Proposition 3.1 decouples the sampling solution from the noise schedule. The solution depends on the starting point О»s and the ending point О»t, and is invariant to the intermediate noise schedule. Similarly, the updating equations of the algorithm of DPM-Solver are also invariant to the intermediate noise schedule. Therefore, if we have chosen the time steps {О»i}M i=0, then the solution of DPM-Solver is also determined and is invariant to the intermediate noise schedule. A simple way for choosing time steps for О»is uniformly splitting [О»T,О»Пө], which is the setting in our experiments. However, we believe that there exists more precise ways for choosing the time steps, and we leave it for future work. A.3 Relationship with the Maximum Likelihood Training of Diffusion Models Interestingly, the maximum likelihood training of diffusion SDEs in continuous time also has such invariance property [10]. Compared to Proposition 3.1, we can п¬Ғnd that the sampling and the maximum likelihood training of diffusion models can both be converted to an integral w.r.t.О», such that the formulation is invariant to the speciп¬Ғc noise schedules, and we summarize it in Table 2. Such invariance property for both training and sampling brings a new insight for understanding diffusion models. For instance, we can directly deп¬Ғne the noise prediction model ПөОё w.r.t. the (half-)logSNR О»instead of the time t, then the training and sampling for diffusion models can be done without further choosing any ad-hoc noise schedules . Such п¬Ғnding may unify the different ways of the training and the inference of diffusion models, and we leave it for future study. B.6 Connections to Explicit Exponential Runge-Kutta (expRK) Methods Assume we have an ODE with the following form: dxt dt = Оұxt + N(xt,t), 18where ОұвҲҲR and N(xt,t) вҲҲRD is a non-linear function of xt. Given an initial value xt at time t, for h> 0, the true solution at time t+ his xt+h = eОұhxt + eОұh вҲ« h 0 eвҲ’ОұП„N(xt+П„,t + П„)dП„. The exponential Runge-Kutta methods [ 25, 31] use some intermediate points to approximate the integral вҲ« eвҲ’ОұП„N(xt+П„,t + П„)dП„. Our proposed DPM-Solver is inspired by the same technique for approximating the same integral with Оұ= 1 and N = ЛңПөОё. However, DPM-Solver is different from the expRK methods, because their linear term eОұhxt is different from our linear term Оұt+h Оұt xt. In summary, DPM-Solver is inspired by the same technique of expRK for deriving high-order approximations of the exponentially weighted integral, but the formulation of DPM-Solver is different from expRK, and DPM-Solver is customized for the speciп¬Ғc formulation of diffusion ODEs. Practically, the training and evaluation for the noise prediction model ПөОё(xt,t) usually start from time T to time Пөto avoid numerical issues for tnear to 0, where Пө> 0 is a hyperparameter [3]. In contrast to the sampling methods based on diffusion SDEs [2, 3], We donвҖҷt add the вҖңdenoisingвҖқ trick at the п¬Ғnal step at time Пө(which is to set the noise variance to zero), and we just solve diffusion ODEs from T to Пөby DPM-Solver, since we п¬Ғnd it performs well enough. For discrete-time DPMs, we п¬Ғrstly convert the model to continuous time (see Appendix D.2), and then solver it from time T to time t. 20Algorithm 7 (DPM-Solver-23) Adaptive step size algorithm by combining DPM-Solver-2 and 3. Discrete-time DPMs [2] train the noise prediction model at N п¬Ғxed time steps {tn}N n=1. In practice, N = 1000 or N = 4000, and the implementation of the 4000-step DPMs [ 16] converts the time steps of 4000-step DPMs to the range of 1000-step DPMs. Speciп¬Ғcally, the noise prediction model is parameterized by ЛңПөОё(xn,1000n N ) for n = 0,...,N вҲ’1, where each xn is corresponding to the value at time tn+1. In practice, these discrete-time DPMs usually choose uniform time steps between [0,T], thus tn = nT N , for n= 1,...,N . However, the discrete-time noise prediction model cannot predict the noise at time less than the smallest time t1. As the smallest time step t1 = T N and the corresponding discrete-time noise prediction model at time t1 is ЛңПөОё(x0,0), we need to вҖңscaleвҖқ the discrete time steps [t1,tN] = [ T N,T] to the continuous time range [Пө,T]. We propose two types of scaling as following. Type-1. Scale the discrete time steps [t1,tN] = [ T N,T] to the continuous time range [ T N,T], and let ПөОё(В·,t) = ПөОё(В·, T N) for tвҲҲ[Пө, T N]. In this case, we can deп¬Ғne the continuous-time noise prediction model by ПөОё(x,t) = ЛңПөОё ( x,1000 В·max ( tвҲ’T N,0 )) , (D.1) where the continuous time tвҲҲ[Пө, T N] maps to the discrete input 0, and the continuous time T maps to the discrete input 1000(NвҲ’1) N . Type-2. Scale the discrete time steps [t1,tN] = [ T N,T] to the continuous time range [0,T]. In this case, we can deп¬Ғne the continuous-time noise prediction model by ПөОё(x,t) = ЛңПөОё ( x,1000 В·(N вҲ’1)t NT ) , (D.2) where the continuous time 0 maps to the discrete input 0, and the continuous time T maps to the discrete input 1000(NвҲ’1) N . Note that the input time of ЛңПөОё may not be integers, but we п¬Ғnd that the noise prediction model can still work well, and we hypothesize that it is because of the smooth time embeddings (e.g., position embeddings [2]). By such reparameterization, the noise prediction model can adopt the continuous-time steps as input, and thus we can also use DPM-Solver for fast sampling. In practice, we have T = 1 , and the smallest discrete time t1 = 10 вҲ’3. For п¬Ғxed K number of function evaluations, we empirically п¬Ғnd that for small K, the Type-1 with Пө= 10вҲ’3 may have better 21sample quality, and for large K, the Type-2 with Пө= 10вҲ’4 may have better sample quality. We refer to Appendix E for detailed results. D.3 DPM-Solver in 20 Function Evaluations Given a п¬Ғxed budget K вүӨ20 of the number of function evaluations, we uniformly divide the interval [О»T,О»Пө] into M = (вҢҠK/3вҢӢ+ 1) segments, and take M steps to generate samples. The M steps are dependent on the remainder Rof Kmod 3 to make sure the total number of function evaluations is exactly K. вҖў If R= 0, we п¬Ғrstly takeMвҲ’2 steps of DPM-Solver-3, and then take1 step of DPM-Solver-2 and 1 step of DPM-Solver-1. The total number of function evaluations is3В·(K 3 вҲ’1)+2+1 = K. вҖў If R= 1, we п¬Ғrstly take MвҲ’1 steps of DPM-Solver-3 and then take1 step of DPM-Solver-1. The total number of function evaluations is 3 В·(KвҲ’1 3 ) + 1 = K. вҖў If R= 2, we п¬Ғrstly take MвҲ’1 steps of DPM-Solver-3 and then take1 step of DPM-Solver-2. The total number of function evaluations is 3 В·(KвҲ’2 3 ) + 2 = K. We empirically п¬Ғnd that this design of time steps can greatly improve the generation quality, and DPM-Solver can generate comparable samples in 10 steps and high-quality samples in 20 steps. D.4 Analytical Formulation of the function tО»(В·) (the inverse function of О»(t)) The costs of computing tО»(В·) is negligible, because for the noise schedules of Оұt and Пғt used in previous DPMs (вҖңlinearвҖқ and вҖңcosineвҖқ) [2, 16], both О»(t) and its inverse function tО»(В·) have analytic formulations. We mainly consider the variance preserving type here, since it is the most widely-used type. The functions of other types (variance exploding and sub-variance preserving type) can be similarly derived. Linear Noise Schedule [2]. We have log Оұt = вҲ’(ОІ1 вҲ’ОІ0) 4 t2 вҲ’ОІ0 2 t, where ОІ0 = 0.1 and ОІ1 = 20, following [3]. As Пғt = вҲҡ 1 вҲ’Оұ2 t, we can compute О»t analytically. Moreover, the inverse function is tО»(О») = 1 ОІ1 вҲ’ОІ0 (вҲҡ ОІ2 0 + 2(ОІ1 вҲ’ОІ0) log (eвҲ’2О» + 1) вҲ’ОІ0 ) . To reduce the inп¬Ӯuence of numerical issues, we can compute tО» by the following equivalent formula- tion: tО»(О») = 2 log ( eвҲ’2О» + 1 ) вҲҡ ОІ2 0 + 2(ОІ1 вҲ’ОІ0) log (eвҲ’2О» + 1) +ОІ0 . And we solve diffusion ODEs between [Пө,T], where T = 1. Cosine Noise Schedule [16]. Denote log Оұt = log ( cos (ПҖ 2 В·t+ s 1 + s )) вҲ’log ( cos (ПҖ 2 В· s 1 + s )) , where s= 0.008, following [16]. As [16] clipped the derivatives to ensure the numerical stability, we also clip the maximum time T = 0.9946. As Пғt = вҲҡ 1 вҲ’Оұ2 t, we can compute О»t analytically. Moreover, given a п¬Ғxed О», let f(О») = вҲ’1 2 log ( eвҲ’2О» + 1 ) , which computes the corresponding log Оұfor О». Then the inverse function is tО»(О») = 2(1 + s) ПҖ arccos ( ef(О»)+log cos( ПҖs 2(1+s) )) вҲ’s. And we solve diffusion ODEs between [Пө,T], where T = 0.9946. 22D.5 Conditional Sampling by DPM-Solver DPM-Solver can also be used for conditional sampling, with a simple modiп¬Ғcation. The conditional generation needs to sample from the conditional diffusion ODE [3, 4] which includes the conditional noise prediction model. We follow the classiп¬Ғer guidance method [ 4] to deп¬Ғne the conditional noise prediction model as ПөОё(xt,t,y ) := ПөОё(xt,t) вҲ’sВ·ПғtвҲҮxlog pt(y|xt; Оё), where pt(y|xt; Оё) is a pre-trained classiп¬Ғer and sis the classiп¬Ғer guidance scale (default is 1.0). Thus, we can use DPM-Solver to solve this diffusion ODE for fast conditional sampling, as shown in Fig. 1. D.6 Numerical Stability As we need to compute ehi вҲ’1 in the algorithm of DPM-Solver, we follow [10] to use expm1(hi) instead of exp(hi)-1 to improve numerical stability. E Experiment Details We test our method for sampling the most widely-used variance-preserving (VP) type DPMs [1, 2]. In this case, we have Оұ2 t + Пғ2 t = 1 for all t вҲҲ[0,T] and ЛңПғ = 1. In spite of this, our method and theoretical results are general and independent of the choice of the noise schedule Оұt and Пғt. For all experiments, we evaluate DPM-Solver on NVIDIA A40 GPUs. However, the computation resource can be other types of GPU, such as NVIDIA GeForce RTX 2080Ti, because we can tune the batch size for sampling. E.1 Diffusion ODEs w.r.t. For the discrete-time models, we only compare the Type-2 discrete method, and the results of Type-1 are similar. We п¬Ғnd that for NFE less than 20, the proposed fast version (DPM-Solver- fast) is usually better than the single order method, and for larger NFE, DPM-Solver-3 is better than DPM-Solver-2, and DPM-Solver-2 is better than DPM-Solver-1, which matches our proposed convergence rate analysis. llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Introduces DPM-Solver, a dedicated high-order (1st/2nd/3rd) ODE solver for fast, training-free sampling in diffusion probabilistic models (DPMs). Derives an exact solution form for diffusion ODEs exploiting their semi-linear structure, analytically handling the linear term and reducing sampling to an exponentially weighted integral over the noise-prediction network in half-log-SNR (О») space. Provides convergence order guarantees, shows DDIM is a special case of DPM-Solver-1, and demonstrates 4вҖ“16Г— speedups achieving high-quality samples in ~10вҖ“20 NFEs across multiple datasets.
llm_extracted_info.methodology:
- Reformulation: Start from probability flow ODE dxt/dt = f(t)xt + (g^2(t)/(2Пғt)) ОөОё(xt,t). Using variation of constants and change of variable О» = log(Оұ/Пғ), obtain exact solution xt = (Оұt/Оұs) xs вҲ’ Оұt вҲ«_{О»t}^{О»s} e^{-О»} ОөОё(xО»,О») dО», where only the nonlinear integral requires approximation.
- Exponential-integrator-inspired solvers: Construct DPM-Solver-k (k=1,2,3) by Taylor-expanding ОөОё in О» and analytically integrating exponential weights (П•-functions), yielding schemes with k-th order global convergence under regularity assumptions.
  вҖў DPM-Solver-1: Update matches DDIM; exact linear evolution plus one network eval per step.
  вҖў DPM-Solver-2: Midpoint in О» with corrective term; two NFEs/step.
  вҖў DPM-Solver-3: Two intermediate О» points (r1=1/3, r2=2/3) and staged corrections; three NFEs/step.
- Step-size scheduling: (i) Uniform steps in О»; (ii) Adaptive control by pairing orders (DPM-Solver-12, -23) with local error estimates and tolerance (Оөrtol, Оөatol). For tight NFE budgets (вүӨ20), combine mostly 3rd-order steps with 1st/2nd-order to match exact NFE.
- Discrete-time DPMs: Reparameterize discrete ОөМғОё(x,n) into continuous-time ОөОё(x,t) via Type-1/Type-2 mappings; provide analytic inverse tО»(В·) for common linear and cosine schedules.
- Conditional sampling: Incorporate classifier guidance via ОөОё(x,t,y) = ОөОё(x,t) вҲ’ sВ·ПғtвҲҮx log pОё(y|x).
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup:
- Models and implementations: Training-free sampling using pre-trained DPMs; implemented in JAX (continuous-time) and PyTorch (discrete-time). Hardware: NVIDIA A40 GPUs.
- Datasets and models:
  вҖў CIFAR-10 (continuous-time VP-deep, linear schedule) from Song et al.; compare SDE Euler, improved Euler (adaptive), ODE RK45, and DPM-Solver.
  вҖў CIFAR-10 (discrete-time DDPM, linear schedule) from Ho et al.
  вҖў CelebA 64Г—64 (discrete-time DDIM, linear schedule).
  вҖў ImageNet 64Г—64 (discrete-time, cosine schedule) from Nichol & Dhariwal.
  вҖў ImageNet 128Г—128 with classifier guidance (ADM-G) from Dhariwal & Nichol.
  вҖў LSUN Bedroom 256Г—256 (discrete-time ADM).
- Baselines: DDPM, DDIM, Analytic-DDPM, Analytic-DDIM, PNDM, FastDPM, ItГҙ-Taylor, GGDM (training-based), SDE Euler/Improved Euler, ODE RK45, RK2/RK3 (t or О» parameterizations).
- Protocols: Evaluate FID on 50K samples; report NFEs; use uniform О»-steps for small NFE (вүӨ20) and adaptive DPM-Solver-12/23 beyond; choose Оө end-time (10^{-3} or 10^{-4}) per setting. Runtime benchmarks vs DDIM show near-identical per-NFE cost, making NFE reductions translate to wall-clock speedups.
- Key results:
  вҖў CIFAR-10 (cont.): FID 4.70 (10 NFE), 2.87 (20 NFE), outperforming RK45 and SDE baselines at same NFEs.
  вҖў CIFAR-10 (disc.): ~4.65 FID at 12 NFE; beats prior training-free samplers and GGDM (training-based).
  вҖў CelebA 64Г—64: ~3.71 FID at 12 NFE; low FIDs with вүӨ20 NFEs.
  вҖў ImageNet 64Г—64: ~20.0 FID at 12 NFE; ~17.5 FID with ~50 NFEs.
  вҖў ImageNet 128Г—128 (guided): 4.08 FID (12 NFE), 3.52 (20 NFE), surpassing DDIM.
  вҖў LSUN 256Г—256: 2.60 FID (20 NFE), ~1.94 at high NFE; better than DDIM.
  вҖў RK comparisons: DPM-Solver-2/3 outperform RK2/3 at same NFE due to exact linear handling.
llm_extracted_info.limitations:
- Designed for fast sampling; not intended to accelerate exact likelihood evaluation in DPMs.
- Despite large speedups, still slower than single-step generators (e.g., GANs) for strict real-time applications.
- Theoretical guarantees require smoothness/Lipschitz assumptions on ОөОё and its О»-derivatives; performance may degrade if these are violated.
- Only orders up to 3 are developed; higher-order schemes might require many intermediate points and are not explored.
- Experiments focus primarily on image generation; generalization to other modalities (audio, video) not empirically validated here.
- For discrete-to-continuous reparameterization (Type-1/Type-2), choice and Оө end-time can affect performance; guidance for optimal selection is empirical.
- Potential for misuse (e.g., generating deceptive content) is amplified by improved sampling efficiency.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions:
- Develop higher-order (>3) exponential-integrator schemes with practical stage counts and stability for DPMs.
- Design principled, schedule-invariant time-step selection directly in О»-space and improved adaptive controllers tailored to DPM error characteristics.
- Unify training and sampling in О»-space (noise-schedule invariance) and investigate training objectives explicitly defined over О».
- Extend and validate on other modalities (audio, speech, video) and larger-scale text-to-image models; integrate with classifier- and classifier-free guidance robustly.
- Combine with training-based accelerations (e.g., distillation) and dynamic programming approaches for hybrid speed-quality gains.
- Theoretical stability/error analyses under guidance, domain shifts, and non-smooth ОөОё; robustness to model misspecification.
- Explore applications to VE/subVP models, latent diffusion, critically-damped Langevin diffusion, and diffusion-GAN hybrids.
- Hardware-aware implementations and batching strategies for real-time or on-device sampling.

research_study_list.title: DPM-Solver: A Fast ODE Solver for Diffusion Probabilistic Model Sampling in Around 10 Steps
research_study_list.abstract:
Diffusion probabilistic models (DPMs) are emerging powerful generative
models. Despite their high-quality generation performance, DPMs still suffer
from their slow sampling as they generally need hundreds or thousands of
sequential function evaluations (steps) of large neural networks to draw a
sample. Sampling from DPMs can be viewed alternatively as solving the
corresponding diffusion ordinary differential equations (ODEs). In this work,
we propose an exact formulation of the solution of diffusion ODEs. The
formulation analytically computes the linear part of the solution, rather than
leaving all terms to black-box ODE solvers as adopted in previous works. By
applying change-of-variable, the solution can be equivalently simplified to an
exponentially weighted integral of the neural network. Based on our
formulation, we propose DPM-Solver, a fast dedicated high-order solver for
diffusion ODEs with the convergence order guarantee. DPM-Solver is suitable for
both discrete-time and continuous-time DPMs without any further training.
Experimental results show that DPM-Solver can generate high-quality samples in
only 10 to 20 function evaluations on various datasets. We achieve 4.70 FID in
10 function evaluations and 2.87 FID in 20 function evaluations on the CIFAR10
dataset, and a $4\sim 16\times$ speedup compared with previous state-of-the-art
training-free samplers on various datasets.
research_study_list.full_text: DPM-Solver: A Fast ODE Solver for Diffusion Probabilistic Model Sampling in Around 10 Steps Cheng LuвҖ , Yuhao ZhouвҖ , Fan BaoвҖ , Jianfei ChenвҖ вҲ—, Chongxuan LiвҖЎ, Jun ZhuвҖ вҲ— вҖ Dept. of Comp. Sci. & Tech., Institute for AI, BNRist Center, THBI Lab вҖ Tsinghua-Bosch Joint ML Center, Tsinghua University, Beijing, 100084 China вҖЎGaoling School of Artiп¬Ғcial Intelligence, Renmin University of China, вҖЎBeijing Key Laboratory of Big Data Management and Analysis Methods, Beijing, China {lucheng.lc15, yuhaoz.cs}@gmail.com; bf19@mails.tsinghua.edu.cn chongxuanli@ruc.edu.cn; {jianfeic, dcszj}@tsinghua.edu.cn Abstract Diffusion probabilistic models (DPMs) are emerging powerful generative models. Despite their high-quality generation performance, DPMs still suffer from their slow sampling as they generally need hundreds or thousands of sequential function evaluations (steps) of large neural networks to draw a sample. Sampling from DPMs can be viewed alternatively as solving the corresponding diffusion ordinary differential equations (ODEs). In this work, we propose an exact formulation of the solution of diffusion ODEs. The formulation analytically computes the linear part of the solution, rather than leaving all terms to black-box ODE solvers as adopted in previous works. By applying change-of-variable, the solution can be equivalently simpliп¬Ғed to an exponentially weighted integral of the neural network. Based on our formulation, we propose DPM-Solver, a fast dedicated high-order solver for diffusion ODEs with the convergence order guarantee. DPM-Solver is suitable for both discrete-time and continuous-time DPMs without any further training. Experimental results show that DPM-Solver can generate high-quality samples in only 10 to 20 function evaluations on various datasets. We achieve 4.70 FID in 10 function evaluations and 2.87 FID in 20 function evaluations on the CIFAR10 dataset, and a 4 вҲј16Г—speedup compared with previous state-of-the-art training-free samplers on various datasets.2 1 Introduction Diffusion probabilistic models (DPMs) [1вҖ“3] are emerging powerful generative models with promis- ing performance on many tasks, such as image generation [4, 5], video generation [6], text-to-image generation [7], speech synthesis [8, 9] and lossless compression [10]. DPMs are deп¬Ғned by discrete- time random processes [1, 2] or continuous-time stochastic differential equations (SDEs) [3], which learn to gradually remove the noise added to the data points. Compared with the widely-used genera- tive adversarial networks (GANs) [11] and variational auto-encoders (V AEs) [12], DPMs can not only compute exact likelihood [3], but also achieve even better sample quality for image generation [4]. However, to obtain high-quality samples, DPMs usually need hundreds or thousands of sequential steps of large neural network evaluations, thereby resulting in a much slower sampling speed than the single-step GANs or V AEs. Such inefп¬Ғciency is becoming a critical bottleneck for the adoption of DPMs in downstream tasks, leading to an urgent request to design fast samplers for DPMs. вҲ—Corresponding Author. 2Code is available at https://github.com/LuChengTHU/dpm-solver 36th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2022). arXiv:2206.00927v3  [cs.LG]  13 Oct 2022NFE = 10  NFE = 15  NFE = 20  NFE = 100  NFE = 10 (a) DDIM [19] (b) DPM-Solver (ours) Figure 1: Samples by DDIM [19] with 10, 15, 20, 100 number of function evaluations (NFE), and DPM-Solver (ours) with only 10 NFE, using the pre-trained DPMs on ImageNet 256Г—256 with classiп¬Ғer guidance [4]. Existing fast samplers for DPMs can be divided into two categories. The п¬Ғrst category includes knowledge distillation [13, 14] and noise level or sample trajectory learning [15вҖ“18]. Such methods require a possibly expensive training stage before they can be used for efп¬Ғcient sampling. Furthermore, their applicability and п¬Ӯexibility might be limited. It might require nontrivial effort to adapt the method to different models, datasets, and number of sampling steps. The second category consists of training-free [19вҖ“21] samplers, which are suitable for all pre-trained DPMs in a simple plug-and- play manner. Training-free samplers include adopting implicit [ 19] or analytical [ 21] generation process, advanced differential equation (DE) solvers [3, 20, 22вҖ“24] and dynamic programming [18]. However, these methods still require вҲј50 function evaluations [21] to generate high-quality samples (comparable to those generated by plain samplers in about 1000 function evaluations), thereby are still time-consuming. In this work, we bring the efп¬Ғciency of training-free samplers to a new level to produce high-quality samples in the вҖңfew-step samplingвҖқ regime, where the sampling can be done within around 10 steps of sequential function evaluations. We tackle the alternative problem of sampling from DPMs as solving the corresponding diffusion ordinary differential equations (ODEs) of DPMs, and carefully examine the structure of diffusion ODEs. Diffusion ODEs have a semi-linear structure вҖ” they consist of a linear function of the data variable and a nonlinear function parameterized by neural networks. Such structure is omitted in previous training-free samplers [ 3, 20], which directly use black-box DE solvers. To utilize the semi-linear structure, we derive an exact formulation of the solutions of diffusion ODEs by analytically computing the linear part of the solutions, avoiding the corresponding discretization error. Furthermore, by applying change-of-variable, the solutions can be equivalently simpliп¬Ғed to an exponentially weighted integral of the neural network. Such integral is very special and can be efп¬Ғciently approximated by the numerical methods for exponential integrators [25]. Based on our formulation of solutions, we propose DPM-Solver, a fast dedicated solver for diffusion ODEs by approximating the above integral. Speciп¬Ғcally, we propose п¬Ғrst-order, second-order and third-order versions of DPM-Solver with convergence order guarantees. We further propose an adaptive step size schedule for DPM-Solver. In general, DPM-Solver is applicable to both continuous- time and discrete-time DPMs, and also conditional sampling with classiп¬Ғer guidance [ 4]. Fig. 1 demonstrates the speedup performance of a Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models (DDIM) [ 19] baseline and DPM-Solver, which shows that DPM-Solver can generate high-quality samples with as few as 10 function evaluations and is much faster than DDIM on the ImageNet 256x256 dataset [26]. Our additional experimental results show that DPM-Solver can greatly improve the sampling speed of both discrete-time and continuous-time DPMs, and it can achieve excellent sample quality in around 10 function evaluations, which is much faster than all previous training-free samplers of DPMs. 2 Diffusion Probabilistic Models We review diffusion probabilistic models and their associated differential equations in this section. 22.1 Forward Process and Diffusion SDEs Assume that we have a D-dimensional random variable x0 вҲҲRD with an unknown distribution q0(x0). Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DPMs) [1вҖ“3, 10] deп¬Ғne a forward process {xt}tвҲҲ[0,T] with T >0 starting with x0, such that for any tвҲҲ[0,T], the distribution of xt conditioned on x0 satisп¬Ғes q0t(xt|x0) = N(xt|Оұ(t)x0,Пғ2(t)I), (2.1) where Оұ(t),Пғ(t) вҲҲR+ are differentiable functions of twith bounded derivatives, and we denote them as Оұt,Пғt for simplicity. The choice for Оұt and Пғt is referred to as the noise schedule of a DPM. Let qt(xt) denote the marginal distribution of xt, DPMs choose noise schedules to ensure that qT(xT) вүҲN(xT|0,ЛңПғ2I) for some ЛңПғ >0, and the signal-to-noise-ratio (SNR) Оұ2 t/Пғ2 t is strictly decreasing w.r.t. t[10]. The only unknown term in Eq. (2.4) is the score function вҲҮxlog qt(xt) at each time t. In practice, DPMs use a neural network ПөОё(xt,t) parameterized by Оё to estimate the scaled score function: вҲ’ПғtвҲҮxlog qt(xt). The parameter Оё is optimized by minimizing the following objective [2, 3]: L(Оё; Пү(t)) := 1 2 вҲ« T 0 Пү(t)Eqt(xt) [ вҲҘПөОё(xt,t) + ПғtвҲҮxlog qt(xt)вҲҘ2 2 ] dt = 1 2 вҲ« T 0 Пү(t)Eq0(x0)Eq(Пө) [ вҲҘПөОё(xt,t) вҲ’ПөвҲҘ2 2 ] dt+ C, where Пү(t) is a weighting function, ПөвҲјq(Пө) = N(Пө|0,I), xt = Оұtx0 + ПғtПө, and C is a constant independent of Оё. As ПөОё(xt,t) can also be regarded as predicting the Gaussian noise added to xt, it is usually called the noise prediction model. (2.5) Samples can be generated from DPMs by solving the diffusion SDE in Eq. (2.5) with numerical solvers, which discretize the SDE from T to 0. Song et al. [3] proved that the traditional ancestral sampling method for DPMs [2] can be viewed as a п¬Ғrst-order SDE solver for Eq.(2.5). However, these п¬Ғrst-order methods usually need hundreds of or thousands of function evaluations to converge [3], leading to extremely slow sampling speed. 2.2 Diffusion (Probability Flow) ODEs When discretizing SDEs, the step size is limited by the randomness of the Wiener process [27, Chap. 11]. A large step size (small number of steps) often causes non-convergence, especially in high dimensional spaces. For faster sampling, one can consider the associated probability п¬Ӯow ODE [3], which has the same marginal distribution at each time tas that of the SDE. Speciп¬Ғcally, for DPMs, Song et al. [3] proved that the probability п¬Ӯow ODE of Eq. (2.4) is dxt dt = f(t)xt вҲ’1 2g2(t)вҲҮxlog qt(xt), xT вҲјqT(xT), (2.6) 3where the marginal distribution of xt is also qt(xt). By replacing the score function with the noise prediction model, Song et al. [3] deп¬Ғned the following parameterized ODE (diffusion ODE): dxt dt = hОё(xt,t) := f(t)xt + g2(t) 2Пғt ПөОё(xt,t), xT вҲјN(0,ЛңПғ2I). (2.7) Samples can be drawn by solving the ODE from T to 0. Comparing with SDEs, ODEs can be solved with larger step sizes as they have no randomness. Furthermore, we can take advantage of efп¬Ғcient numerical ODE solvers to accelerate the sampling. Song et al. [3] used the RK45 ODE solver [28] for the diffusion ODEs, which generates samples in вҲј60 function evaluations to reach comparable quality with a 1000-step SDE solver for Eq. (2.5) on the CIFAR-10 dataset [29]. However, existing general-purpose ODE solvers still cannot generate satisfactory samples in the few-step (вҲј10 steps) sampling regime. To the best of our knowledge, there is still a lack of training-free samplers for DPMs in the few-step sampling regime, and the sampling speed of DPMs is still a critical issue. 3 Customized Fast Solvers for Diffusion ODEs As highlighted in Sec. 2.2, discretizing SDEs is generally difп¬Ғcult in high dimensions [27, Chap. 11] and it is hard to converge within few steps. In contrast, ODEs are easier to solve, yielding a potential for fast samplers. However, as mentioned in Sec. 2.2, the general black-box ODE solver used in previous work [3] empirically fails to converge in few steps. This motivates us to design a dedicated solver for diffusion ODEs to enable fast and high-quality few-step sampling. Therefore, we only consider kfrom 1 to 3 in this work, while leaving the solvers with higher kfor future study. 63.3 Step Size Schedule The proposed solvers in Sec. 3.2 need to specify the time steps {ti}M i=0 in advance. We propose two choices of the time step schedule. One choice is handcrafted, which is to uniformly split the interval [О»T, О»0], i.e. О»ti = О»T + i M(О»0 вҲ’О»T), i = 0,...,M . Note that this is different from previous work [2, 3] which chooses uniform steps for ti. Empirically, DPM-Solver with uniform time steps О»ti can already generate quite good samples in few steps, where results are listed in Appendix E. As the other choice, we propose an adaptive step size algorithm, which dynamically adjusts the step size by combining different orders of DPM-Solver. The adaptive algorithm is inspired by [20] and we defer its implementation details to Appendix C. For few-step sampling, we need to use up all the number of function evaluations (NFE). When the NFE is not divisible by 3, we п¬Ғrstly apply DPM-Solver-3 as much as possible, and then add a single step of DPM-Solver-1 or DPM-Solver-2 (dependent on the reminder of Kdivided by 3), as detailed in Appendix D. In the subsequent experiments, we use such combination of solvers with the uniform step size schedule for NFE вүӨ20, and otherwise the adaptive step size schedule. 3.4 Sampling from Discrete-Time DPMs Discrete-time DPMs [2] train the noise prediction model at N п¬Ғxed time steps {tn}N n=1, and the noise prediction model is parameterized by ЛңПөОё(xn,n) for n = 0 ,...,N вҲ’1, where each xn is corresponding to the value at timetn+1. We can transform the discrete-time noise prediction model to the continuous version by letting ПөОё(x,t) := ЛңПөОё(x,(NвҲ’1)t T ), for all xвҲҲRd,t вҲҲ[0,T]. Note that the input time of ЛңПөОё may not be integers, but we п¬Ғnd that the noise prediction model can still work well, and we hypothesize that it is because of the smooth time embeddings (e.g., position embeddings [2]). By such reparameterization, the noise prediction model can adopt the continuous-time steps as input, and thus we can also use DPM-Solver for fast sampling. 4 Comparison with Existing Fast Sampling Methods Here, we discuss the relationship and highlight the difference between DPM-Solver and existing ODE-based fast sampling methods for DPMs. We further brieп¬Ӯy discuss the advantage of training-free samplers over those training-based ones. 4.1 DDIM as DPM-Solver-1 Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models (DDIM) [19] design a deterministic method for fast sampling from DPMs. For two adjacent time steps tiвҲ’1 and ti, assume that we have a solution ЛңxtiвҲ’1 at time tiвҲ’1, then a single step of DDIM from time tiвҲ’1 to time ti is Лңxti = Оұti ОұtiвҲ’1 ЛңxtiвҲ’1 вҲ’Оұti (ПғtiвҲ’1 ОұtiвҲ’1 вҲ’Пғti Оұti ) ПөОё(ЛңxtiвҲ’1 ,tiвҲ’1). (4.1) Although motivated by entirely different perspectives, we show that the updates of DPM-Solver-1 and Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models (DDIM) [19] are identical. By the deп¬Ғnition of О», we have ПғtiвҲ’1 ОұtiвҲ’1 = eвҲ’О»tiвҲ’1 and Пғti Оұti = eвҲ’О»ti. Plugging these and hi = О»tiвҲ’О»tiвҲ’1 to Eq. (4.1) results in exactly a step of DPM-Solver-1 in Eq. (3.7). However, the semi-linear ODE formulation of DPM-Solver allows for principled generalization to higher-order solvers and convergence order analysis. Recent work [13] also show that DDIM is a п¬Ғrst-order discretization of diffusion ODEs by differenti- ating both sides of Eq. (4.1). However, they cannot explain the difference between DDIM and the п¬Ғrst-order Euler discretization of diffusion ODEs. In contrast, by showing that DDIM is a special case of DPM-Solver, we reveal that DDIM makes full use of the semi-linearity of diffusion ODEs, which explains its superiority over traditional Euler methods. 4.2 Comparison with Traditional Runge-Kutta Methods One can obtain a high-order solver by directly applying traditional explicit Runge-Kutta (RK) methods to the diffusion ODE in Eq. (2.7). Speciп¬Ғcally, RK methods write the solution of Eq.
